policy_statement: Amend State building code to account for sea level rise and enhanced riverine flooding and allow innovative structures such as amphibious buildings (DOS).
mechanism_description: The state plans to amend the building code to account for sea level rise and increased riverine flooding and to allow innovative designs such as amphibious buildings, reducing future flood damage risk.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (sea level rise/riverine flooding) → building design/standards reduce exposure & vulnerability → ↓ flood damages & service disruption
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Building design, siting, elevation, floodproofing set, and permitted typologies (e.g., amphibious buildings) are updated via code to account for projected flood conditions. (2) Mediating process: Improved structural resilience and/or avoidance of inundation (elevation, buoyancy/amphibious response, wet/dry floodproofing) reduces the probability and severity of floodwater intrusion and structural failure under higher flood levels. (3) Outcome: Lower expected flood losses (property damage, displacement, downtime) and improved continuity of building function under climate-amplified flooding.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood damage and disruption by lowering buildings’ exposure/vulnerability to increased flooding hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: addresses a climate hazard (increased flooding) by changing building standards/design to reduce exposure and vulnerability, yielding reduced damages—an adaptation pathway. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: while resilient designs can sometimes affect energy use or embodied carbon, the stated and principal effect is flood risk reduction, not emissions reduction or resource efficiency. 3) Edge cases applied: if a code update also mandates low-carbon materials or energy efficiency, those would be separate mitigation/resource-efficiency mechanisms; here the mechanism is explicitly flood-risk-oriented. 4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy defined by “building code update for flood risk → flood damage risk reduction” necessarily targets reduced flood impacts, so Adaptation is consistently primary. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., coastal vs riverine, new build vs retrofit, critical facilities) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some flood-resilience measures can increase embodied carbon (e.g., additional concrete for elevation) or energy use (e.g., dehumidification after flooding); these do not change the primary adaptation classification for this mechanism but may matter for co-benefit/tradeoff assessment.
key_indicators: "Strengthen State building code"; "amend State building code"; "account for sea level rise"; "enhanced riverine flooding"; "innovative structures, such as amphibious buildings"
co_benefits: Potential improved general flood safety and reduced non-climate flood losses; possible insurance/financing benefits from reduced risk (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be framed as a performance-standard if the amendment specifies performance outcomes (e.g., design flood elevation/freeboard) rather than prescriptive rules, but the source text emphasizes code amendment generally, so regulatory-mandate fits best.
